question: What are the responsibilities of a Professional Doctorate Director within the Departmental arrangements for professional doctorates?
expected_answer: The Director is responsible to the Head of Department (HoD) for the departmental arrangements for relevant PD students and for liaising with and reporting as necessary to the Dean. The Director ensures that new PD students are supplied with the details of the departmental arrangements, which should be appropriate for the degree programme concerned and approved by the Dean.
keyphrases: liaising with and reporting as necessary to the Dean
follow_up_question: What should be done if there are any major changes to the departmental arrangements for professional doctorates?
follow_up_expected_answer: If there are any major changes to the departmental arrangements, they will be reviewed every three years or sooner if required due to major changes or updates in University policies, procedures, or regulations.

question: What actions can be considered as academic offences according to the document?
expected_answer: Actions such as plagiarism, self-plagiarism, false authorship or contract cheating, collusion, falsifying data or evidence, unethical academic practice, and unauthorized access during exams are examples of academic offences.
follow_up_question: Can you give an example of unethical academic practice mentioned in the document?
follow_up_expected_answer: Conducting research without obtaining ethical approval from the University where such approval is required, or the unauthorised use of information that has been confidentially acquired are examples of unethical academic practices.

question: What are the additional responsibilities placed upon students enrolled on courses requiring practical professional placements regarding their professional suitability?
expected_answer: Students have additional responsibilities regarding not only their conduct but also their professional suitability as outlined in relevant regulatory and/or professional body codes of practice.
keyphrases: regulatory and/or professional body codes of practice
follow_up_question: What happens if a student fails to meet these additional responsibilities during a practical professional placement?
follow_up_expected_answer: If a student fails to meet these additional responsibilities, the Fitness to Practise Procedure can be invoked.

question: What is the responsibility of the Course Lead in relation to Professional Doctorate students at the Tavistock and Portman NHS Foundation Trust?
expected_answer: The Course Lead is responsible to the Portfolio Manager for the Trust arrangements for relevant PD students, and will liaise with and report as necessary to the Dean.
keyphrases: liaising with and reporting as necessary to the Dean
follow_up_question: What actions should be taken by new Professional Doctorate students after receiving Trust arrangements documentation?
follow_up_expected_answer: During the induction, or within the first three weeks of term, the Course Lead should discuss the details contained in this Code of Practice, the student handbook, and the Trust arrangements relating to them. Students should be given information about expectations and conventions regarding referencing and citations, and be advised about the University’s policy on plagiarism and academic offences.
follow_up_keyphrases: discuss details contained in Code of Practice

question: What must a student provide if their appeal outcome requires continued registration at the University?
expected_answer: Students must provide proof of their right to study with the University for the duration of their course.
keyphrases: proof of their right to study
follow_up_question: What happens if a student’s continued UK immigration status is brought into question during an appeal process?
follow_up_expected_answer: A referral will be made to the International Services team for consideration in accordance with paragraphs 7.22-7.28 of the University's General Regulations and relevant UK Immigration rules and guidance.
follow_up_keyphrases: referred to the International Services team

question: What happens if an undergraduate student applying for a Student Loans Company (SLC) funding refuses their application and fails to pay the tuition fees?
expected_answer: The student will accept personal liability as a self-funded student, and failure to pay the fees will lead to withdrawal of the student’s registration with the University.
follow_up_question: How are postgraduate students with a confirmed Postgraduate Loan from the Student Loans Company expected to make their fee payments?
follow_up_expected_answer: Postgraduate students will be granted an extension to the normal instalment payment dates, in line with the Postgraduate Loan release dates, and will be invoiced as follows: 33.3̅% of the annual fee on or before certain dates; failure to pay by these dates will lead to withdrawal from registration.

question: What are the consequences of failing to adhere to the Professional Code of Conduct in a module?
expected_answer: Failure to adhere to the PCC in a module may result in an overall fail mark being awarded for the module, which is likely to have implications on a student’s ability to progress to the next academic stage or graduate.
follow_up_question: What are the specific types of assessment criteria that non-attendance can make impossible according to the Professional Code of Conduct?
follow_up_expected_answer: Non-attendance makes attainment of the following types of assessment criteria impossible: a) Ability to work as part of a team, b) Contribution to group work or performance, c) Positive contribution to discussions.

question: What are the principles that guide content development and management at the University of Essex Library & Cultural Services?
expected_answer: Library & Cultural Services works in partnership with its users, prioritizes digital content, supports open research, subscribes to intellectual freedom, and purchases a range of academic and industry-based content.
follow_up_question: How does the library ensure that its content is diverse and inclusive?
follow_up_expected_answer: The library ensures diversity and inclusivity by working in partnership with users to ensure that the content reflects teaching and research requirements, thereby ensuring that the content is diverse and inclusive.

question: What are the five principles underlying the Student Engagement Policy at the University of Essex?
expected_answer: The policy is underpinned by five principles: an approach to monitoring and responding to student engagement that is driven, and seen to be driven, by support and advice to succeed; visibility and a shared understanding across the institution of student engagement and the impact of our interventions; personalized interventions and mutually agreed solutions for students as far as possible, tailored to individual needs and circumstances to support students to be active participants in their education and academic progress; parity of experience for all our students regardless of academic discipline or Department through the establishment of clear institutional expectations both of the University and of our students in managing student engagement; resource deployment that focuses on effectiveness, efficiency and is targeted in support of student success.
keyphrases: driven by support and advice to succeed
parity of experience for all students
follow_up_question: Can you provide an example of how the principle of personalized interventions is applied in the policy?
follow_up_expected_answer: The policy seeks to enable us to identify and understand many single or combined factors that may affect students' engagement with their academic studies and/or their personal circumstances, and intervene in order to provide appropriate support to facilitate our students' success and development to thrive in their future lives.

question: What criteria must candidates applying for a postgraduate research degree involving practice as research demonstrate during admissions?
expected_answer: Candidates must demonstrate proven capacity and experience to deliver the types of research output proposed, along with preparedness for research. An appropriate supervisory team should also be decided upon, including at least one supervisor who has experience in undertaking PaR or supervising PaR students.
follow_up_question: What happens if a student's PaR project requires changes to the approved research output and assessment timeline?
follow_up_expected_answer: Any such changes would need to be approved by the Research Student Progress Board and must take into account feasibility and the impact on the student’s timely completion.
follow_up_keyphrases: approved by the Research Student Progress Board

question: What are the main considerations when determining the most suitable viva format according to the document?
expected_answer: Video Link Vivas provide the ability to expand the network of external examiners internationally as potential barriers, such as financial constraints, time commitments and our environmental efforts to reduce our carbon footprint, are removed. There are both financial and time-saving benefits for all viva attendees.
follow_up_question: What specific barriers can be reduced by using Video Link Vivas?
follow_up_expected_answer: Specific barriers that can be reduced include financial constraints, time commitments, and the University's environmental efforts to reduce its carbon footprint.

question: What are the payment terms for self-funded International fee-paying students starting their course in academic year 2024-25 who opt not to pay their tuition fees in full?
expected_answer: Self-funded International fee-paying students starting their course in academic year 2024-25, who opt not to pay their tuition fees in full, are required to pay fees in two equal instalments, payable in October and January (for October intake), in January and April (for January intake) or in April and October (for April intake).
follow_up_question: What happens if a student does not make the required payments for their tuition fees according to the specified terms?
follow_up_expected_answer: Where a student has Education Debt and the University has not been able to recover sums, non-payment of Education Debt may prevent a student from continuing their studies or from receiving an academic award.
follow_up_keyphrases: preventing a student from continuing their studies

question: What are the conditions for postgraduate research students to apply for GTA or GLA positions?
expected_answer: Postgraduate research students are eligible to apply for GTA/GLA posts except when they are in further completion or an extension to completion period. Research supervisors need to agree that progress is good and that students can apply or continue to apply for GTA roles and agree the number of hours to be worked.
follow_up_question: Are there any specific restrictions on the number of hours a postgraduate student can work as a GTA or GLA?
follow_up_expected_answer: Any restrictions on hours set out in the University’s Principal Regulations for Research Degrees, RCUK training grant or any other scholarship will apply.

question: Which specific referencing styles and versions can be used by departments and schools at the University of Essex?
expected_answer: Departments and Schools should select a single referencing style and version from a limited list maintained by Library and Cultural Services. The selection is optional for postgraduate research cohorts, but a broad approach to referencing education must still be embedded.
follow_up_question: Can you provide an example of one of the allowed referencing styles?
follow_up_expected_answer: Yes, examples include APA, MLA, or Chicago, but only specific versions from these are permitted.

question: What should students do if they wish to raise a complaint related to the provision of continuity for their course of study?
expected_answer: Students should deal with their complaints through the Student Concerns and Complaints Procedure.
follow_up_question: What are possible outcomes if a student's complaint is upheld under this procedure?
follow_up_expected_answer: Possible remedies for the student include an apology, explanation, and/or practical remedies wherever possible.

question: How many members of staff must be nominated by a department if they have more than 149 postgraduate research students for potential Independent Chairs?
expected_answer: If a department has more than 149 postgraduate research students, they must nominate 3 members of staff to be listed as potential Independent Chairs.
follow_up_question: What happens if a department has fewer than the minimum number of nominations required for their PGR student community?
follow_up_expected_answer: The Faculty Education Committee approves the list of potential Independent Chairs annually, so departments will be notified by the Postgraduate Research Education Team how many nominations they must put forward.

question: What does QAA define dual awards as in the policy document?
expected_answer: Dual awards are defined as two degree-awarding bodies jointly designing a course of study comprising a joint initial curriculum leading to two separate qualifications awarded individually by the two degree-awarding bodies.
follow_up_question: Which components are necessary for obtaining a joint award according to the document?
follow_up_expected_answer: All components must be successfully completed and lead to a single joint award.

question: How long can a Postgraduate Research Degree (PGR) student request compassionate leave under this policy?
expected_answer: The PGR student can request up to two weeks of compassionate leave, which is equivalent to 10 working days.
follow_up_question: What happens when a PGR student returns to their studies after taking compassionate leave?
follow_up_expected_answer: When the PGR student returns, the supervisor should discuss any additional support needs and make referrals to appropriate support services as needed.

question: What do Masters of Philosophy wear for academic dress?
expected_answer: Masters of Philosophy wear a black gown, similar to other Masters, a hood of full shape with the outside University red and fully lined with white inside, and a black mortar-board.
follow_up_question: How is the academic dress for Honorary Doctors different from PhDs?
follow_up_expected_answer: Honorary Doctors wear a University red gown with black cuffs and facings, while PhDs have maroon sleeves and a black bonnet.

question: What happens if a student's work is sponsored by a commercial organization?
expected_answer: In such cases, the University’s Research and Enterprise Office helps draw up a contractual agreement with the commercial organisation concerned which will cover any special intellectual property provisions.
follow_up_question: Who is responsible for drawing up the agreement in case of sponsorship by a commercial organization?
follow_up_expected_answer: The University’s Research and Enterprise Office helps draw up the agreement.

question: What is the minimum Year Mark required for a student to be eligible for a Term Abroad in an Essex Abroad course?
expected_answer: A minimum Year Mark of 50 in Year One must be achieved. If during Year One, you undertook reassessment, the Uncapped Year Mark will be used.
follow_up_question: What happens if a student does not meet this requirement for the Term Abroad?
follow_up_expected_answer: The document does not specify what happens if a student does not meet the minimum Year Mark requirement.

question: What is the minimum Year Mark required for a student to be eligible for a Term Abroad under the Essex Abroad rules in the academic year 2025-26?
expected_answer: To be eligible for a Term Abroad, students must achieve a minimum Year Mark of 50 in Year One. If they undertook reassessment during Year One, the Uncapped Year Mark will be used to determine eligibility.
follow_up_question: What happens if a student fails to meet the minimum Year Mark requirement for a Term Abroad?
follow_up_expected_answer: Students who do not meet the minimum Year Mark of 50 in Year One will not be eligible for a Term Abroad. They must achieve the required mark before applying.

question: What is the minimum Year Mark required for a student to be eligible for a Term Abroad in Joint courses involving Modern Language/Language Studies at University of Essex?
expected_answer: A minimum Year Mark of 50 in Year One is required to be eligible for a Term Abroad.
follow_up_question: What happens if during Year One, a student undertakes reassessment for Joint courses involving Modern Language/Language Studies?
follow_up_expected_answer: If during Year One, the student undertakes reassessment, the Uncapped Year Mark will be used to determine eligibility for a Term Abroad.

question: What is the pass mark for all modules of the Graduate Diploma according to these rules?
expected_answer: The pass mark for all modules of the Graduate Diploma is 40.
follow_up_question: How are credits rounded in cases where a student scores exactly halfway between two whole numbers?
follow_up_expected_answer: For the purposes of these rules, taught module averages and the overall weighted average for the taught module will be rounded to the nearest whole number – i.e. 39.5 will count as 40, 39.4 as 39.

question: What are the specific requirements for a student to achieve a Distinction in Year One under the rules stated in this document?
expected_answer: To achieve a Distinction in Year One, a student must have either: (a) A Year Mark of 70 or above; or (b) 60 Credits at 70 or above with a Year Mark of 68 or 69.
follow_up_question: What happens if a student does not meet the requirements to progress from Year One under these rules?
follow_up_expected_answer: If a student does not meet the progression requirements, they will be considered for transfer to an Honours Degree programme or must undertake necessary reassessment prior to the next academic year.

question: What are the requirements for a student to achieve a Distinction in Year One of the Integrated Masters programme in Modern Languages (Translation)?
expected_answer: To achieve a Distinction, students must have either: A Year Mark of 70 or above; or 60 Credits at 70 or above with a Year Mark of 68 or 69.
follow_up_question: What happens if a student fails to meet the requirements for progression but has passed more than 30 credits in Year One?
follow_up_expected_answer: Students must undertake necessary reassessment prior to the next Academic Year in order to fulfil the requirement to progress. Marks will be capped at 40.

question: What is the maximum number of non-core credits that can be failed in Year One and still pass to the Final Year under the University of Essex Foundation Degree rules?
expected_answer: You can fail a maximum of 15 non-core credits across your degree, so if you fail 15 non-core credits in Year One, you cannot fail any more credits in your degree.
follow_up_question: What happens if a student fails more than 15 non-core credits in Year One?
follow_up_expected_answer: If a student fails more than 15 non-core credits in Year One, they will not be able to continue their studies under the University of Essex Foundation Degree rules.

question: What is the requirement for passing Year One according to the rules of assessment?
expected_answer: To pass Year One and proceed to Year Two, students must pass all modules and achieve 120 credits in the Year at Level 4.
follow_up_question: What happens if a student does not pass all modules but still meets the credit requirement?
follow_up_expected_answer: The specific rules for such scenarios are not detailed in this document, and the process may involve reassessment or other measures as outlined in subsequent sections.

question: What is the maximum number of non-core credits that can be condoned across a three-year Honours Degree at Essex University?
expected_answer: The maximum number of non-core credits that can be condoned across a Three-Year Honours Degree is 15.
follow_up_question: How are the failed non-core credits recorded if they are not condoned or trailed?
follow_up_expected_answer: Failed non-core credits that are neither condoned nor trailed will be recorded in the final year’s marks and affect the calculation of the final degree classification.

question: What are the consequences for students who fail to complete the required placement hours in EG124, EG226, or EG229 modules?
expected_answer: If a student fails to complete the required placement hours by the end of Week 36, they will receive a FAIL for this element and cannot undertake the Professional Performance Assessment. They may be offered reassessment in the next academic year if they have failed the placement element.
follow_up_question: What is the process for obtaining in-person reassessment for a student who has not completed their relevant placement hours?
follow_up_expected_answer: Except where there are accepted Extenuating Circumstances, in-person reassessment without attendance or trailing reassessment in the next Academic Year can only be offered where the student has completed their placement hours but have failed to achieve a Pass Mark of 40.

question: What is the minimum pass mark for modules in Year Four of the MSci Nursing programme?
expected_answer: The pass mark for modules in Year Four is 50.
follow_up_question: What happens if a student fails an assessment on their final attempt without valid extenuating circumstances?
follow_up_expected_answer: Students will be required to withdraw from the programme.

question: What is the pass mark required for the HS831 linguistics and phonetics exam, and how is it adjusted to reflect a standard pass mark of 50?
expected_answer: The pass mark required for the HS831 linguistics and phonetics exam is at least 74. The 74 examination pass mark will be adjusted proportionately to represent in real terms the mark students would achieve if the module used the standard pass mark of 50 that applies to other modules.
follow_up_question: How is the transformed grade calculated for students who achieve a raw grade below the adjusted pass mark in HS831?
follow_up_expected_answer: The transformed grade is achieved by applying the formula: Transformed grade = 1.92 x [raw grade – 74] + 50.

department: School of Sport, Rehabilitation and Exercise Science
question: What are the criteria for a student to receive credit from taught modules under these rules?
expected_answer: A core module must be taken as part of the programme structure and passed, with no condonement of marks below a pass allowed. Credits are only awarded if the module is passed.
follow_up_question: Which modules require students to achieve specific pass marks beyond the standard 50%?
follow_up_expected_answer: For HS632 Preparation for Independent and Supplementary Prescribing V300 (NMC/HCPC) Pharmacology Examination, a student must achieve a pass mark of 80. For SE708 (and the alternative SE746) the MCQ exam pass mark is 70%.

question: What are the credit limits for condonable non-core modules at each level?
expected_answer: The credit limits are as follows: The MRes limit is 60; the Masters is 40; the Postgraduate Diploma is 30; the Postgraduate Certificate is 20.
follow_up_question: How many credits must be passed to achieve a pass in the Masters degree according to the MRes Rules of Assessment?
follow_up_expected_answer: To be awarded a pass in the Masters, a student should attempt 360 credits at Masters Level (Level 7) of which 300 must be passed, with the remaining credits condoned, subject to (c).

question: What are the criteria for a student to be eligible for an award of a pass in the Graduate Certificate program?
expected_answer: To be awarded a pass in the GRADUATE CERTIFICATE, a student should obtain 60 credits; pass all the taught core modules; pass all assessments; and meet all the attendance requirements for the programme.
keyphrases: pass all the taught core modules
follow_up_question: What happens if a student fails a taught module in the Graduate Certificate program?
follow_up_expected_answer: Students who have failed any taught module may have a second attempt at the assessment for the module(s).

question: What are the progression requirements for students in Essex Business School's Integrated PhD program under Model A?
expected_answer: To pass Year One and proceed to Year Two of the Integrated PhD, a student must: attempt 180 credits at Masters level (level 7), pass all taught core modules with a mark of 60 or more, condone failure in up to 40 credits in non-core taught modules as long as the overall weighted average is 50 or above, pass the dissertation or equivalent with a mark of 65 or more, and achieve an overall weighted average mark of 65 or more for all taught modules and the dissertation.
keyphrases: pass all taught core modules with a mark of 60 or more
up to 40 credits in non-core taught modules
overall weighted average mark of 65 or more
follow_up_question: What happens if a student fails a core module but still meets the overall weighted average requirement?
follow_up_expected_answer: The student will be offered reassessment in the individual core module where they did not meet the progression requirements, and the marks achieved after reassessment will be used to determine eligibility to progress to Year Two of the Integrated PhD.

question: What criteria must be met to achieve a pass, merit, and distinction in the Masters degrees under these rules?
expected_answer: To achieve a pass, students need an overall weighted average of at least 50. For a Merit, they should have an overall weighted average mark of 60 or more. For a Distinction, they must have an overall weighted average mark of 70 or more.
follow_up_question: How are credits and marks handled when a student fails with a mark between 40-49.5?
follow_up_expected_answer: Failure with a mark of 40 or more for up to 40 credits in non-core taught modules may be condoned provided the overall weighted average for all the taught modules is 50.

question: What must be ensured by Heads of Department regarding student progress monitoring, as per the document?
expected_answer: Heads of Department are responsible for ensuring that an effective means of monitoring students' progress and attendance is established and maintained in each department.
follow_up_question: What should be communicated to all students by Heads of Department regarding their progress monitoring procedures?
follow_up_expected_answer: Departmental procedures, including norms for formal face-to-face meetings between students and their supervisor(s), should be communicated to all students in the Department.

question: What is the expected time frame for the return of assessed work according to the University's policy?
expected_answer: The expectation for the return of assessed work should be within 20 working days.
follow_up_question: What should departments do if they are unable to meet their stated deadline for providing feedback?
follow_up_expected_answer: Departments should inform students of this and advise them of the revised arrangements.

question: What is the submission deadline for candidates first registered from October 2018 onwards?
expected_answer: The submission deadline of the thesis for examination is at the end of the standard period of study.
follow_up_question: What happens if a candidate requires an extended completion period beyond the standard period?
follow_up_expected_answer: If deemed necessary by the Supervisory Panel and Research Students’ Progress Board, a one, two or three-term period of completion may be recommended to the relevant Faculty Dean for approval.
